author: @buckshot6554
text:
Robert, I would love to see a video about the “old tech” solar panels invented by George Cove.



Apparently George was trying to make a thermoelectric (Seebeck)  device, and he accidentally made a photoelectric device.  Also,  solar panels made from George’s “old tech” are potentially DIY, and they would be far more environmentally friendly than current silicon PV panels.



Thank you so much for sharing your knowledge, passion, and wisdom!

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: This is a subject I’ve often pondered on Robbie. Bear with me for a mo…..I wouldn’t call radiator heat ‘wasted’ as the rad is obviously designed to do (radiate) exactly that BUT, as you know, heat rises and so we all live in houses that, although they may be warm (if the boiler is on) the temp at ceiling height is greater than what we feel at a normal working/standing/sitting height. If your ceiling joists have insulation between them then that heat will remain there for a lot longer than if no insulation exists. It’s actually surprising just how much heat ‘hangs’ around at that height - fit a ceiling fan and you will immediately feel the difference as the warm, higher air is forced down and distributed to the cooler, lower areas. In the worst example a complete lack of insulation means that your heated air simply rises to roof level and then out into the atmosphere……what a waste!. Our ceilings, although not low, are not particularly suited to have such big fans dotted around the house and so we don’t get the potential benefit of their use……..what we do have is a heat recovery system which utilises this higher temp air to heat ALL of our hot water (via 2 high level input ducts) and so it bugs me to think that although I may be comfortably warm (whilst watching your vids on the iPad, led flat out on the sofa 🙂) I’m actually heating my ceilings to a greater extent……..wouldn’t it be great if there existed some background, perhaps Peltier-driven, smaller fan systems that circulated and mixed the higher warmer air into the lower cooler air. Not only would we get the benefit of a higher room temp but it could also result in energy saving as the thermostat could then be turned down too. Thanks for the vid! 👍

replies.author: @rogerwilson6367
replies.text: In theory, moving the air around a room by forced convection could lead to incresed conduction losses through walls etc. Turbulent air flow increases conduction. Just a thought.

author: @nickwinn7812
text: A lot of waste energy from a radiator? All of the heat energy coming from a radiator goes into the room in which it stands, so where is the waste? I think you should think this through. You are simply taking some of your gas  (or other primary fuel) energy and turning it into electricity by fisrt burnig the gas and then using the peltier effect to convert the heat to electricity, thus two processes with their own separate in-efficiencies. Better to generate the electricity from the primary fuel source in the first place I think. I love your projects but please don't start misleading folks into thinking they can get "free" energy in any way shape or form. Fit the peltier device on the flue of the gas appliance and recuperate some of the otherwise lost (and expensive) energy. Not free, but more efficient use of the fuel you've burnt. Sorry, you don't mention free energy but it was implied I think.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Excellent!  
It seems like this could be developed- even the electric radiator- into something quite a bit more efficient than the norm.  Like if you had your radiator in an insulated box, put your peltier's on there,  with your heat sink on the Outside, you'd still get your heat into the room, but all of it would be reclaiming electricity as it went.     Seems like you could also do that with an AC unit in a warm humid place- blow your hot exhaust on the peltier, and route the condensation that always happens across the other side of it.  That could Really improve the efficiency of both heating and cooling, I think. 
Sterling also seems an option to play with either way- does anybody know,  Could you get more electricity out of a peltier, or a stirling engine running a generator?  google was not forthcoming with the answer to that.

author: @spongobobby
text: Dear Robert, I have been building one of those Peltier devices some years ago for my wood stove to distribute the warm air into areas where it is not going by itself. It basically consists of an old grounding magnet ( for easy mounting on magnetic surfaces) thermal grease, a peltier element and a cooler from an old CPU. The initial power generated when heating up is used to run a first fan and a LED. This fan is sucking cool air through the system increasing the difference in temperature while the stove is still heating up. When the stove is on "working temperature" i can switch two additional fans on and they start blowing hot air into another room. I was as impressed as you are by the idea of using excess energy to generate electricity. Though I don see that a radiator produces excess energy, the tiny amount you "redirect" to produces electricity wont give you cold feet ;) Greetings from Germany. I really like your videos.
